title: The EU Must Confront China’s Trade Challenge - The Wire China
gist: A MERICS analyst argues the EU is losing a race against time on China trade: it keeps deferring confrontation over the 'China Shock 2.0' export flood while Beijing rapidly builds supply-chain defenses and coercion tools, and looming 2027 European elections (especially France) threaten to dissolve the bloc's appetite for collective action.

themes.theme: EU delay vs. the China Shock 2.0

insights.claim: At their mid-June 2026 European Council, EU leaders again chose preparation over confrontation on China, deferring concrete steps and only agreeing to strengthen the existing trade-defense 'toolbox', with the discussion pushed to October.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Repeated EU deferral means no near-term tariff or market-access shock to Chinese exporters, but a building backlog of measures that could land later in 2026.
insights.quote: they once again chose preparation over confrontation.

insights.claim: The EU is estimated to be losing around 500 manufacturing jobs a day as Chinese imports pressure its higher-value sectors.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Quantifies the political clock: continued job erosion feeds populist/Eurosceptic parties, which would further paralyze collective China policy.
insights.quote: Some estimates suggest that the EU is losing around 500 manufacturing jobs a day as Chinese imports put pressure on Europe’s higher-value sectors.
insights.locator: Failing to respond to the flood of China’s exports

insights.claim: The longer the EU delays, the stronger China's economic defenses become and the more industrial capacity Europe loses, narrowing the EU's options and raising the eventual cost.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Time works against the EU's leverage, so the trajectory points to a weaker eventual European position vis-a-vis Chinese exporters.
insights.quote: The longer the EU waits, the narrower its options — and the higher the eventual price.
insights.locator: The June European Council should have been a starting point

insights.claim: France's April 2027 presidential election is the most consequential deadline because under Macron, Paris has driven assertive EU China policy (EV tariffs, economic-security tools), a role that could fade if the far-right Rassemblement National wins.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A 2027 French political shift is a concrete catalyst that could collapse EU collective China policy; a key date for positioning around EU-China trade risk.
insights.quote: The most immediate and consequential deadline is France’s April 2027 presidential election.
insights.locator: The most immediate and consequential deadline

insights.claim: RN candidate Jordan Bardella has supported de-risking and trade defense but called the EU 'completely obsolete', signaling he would favor national over European solutions.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Even a China-hawk RN could weaken bloc-level coordination, fragmenting the EU response into less effective national measures.
insights.quote: he has also called the EU “completely obsolete”, signaling that he is likely to favor national over European solutions.
insights.locator: But he has also called the EU

insights.claim: Looming elections in Italy, Spain and Poland are also set to weaken those countries' appetite for bold EU initiatives on China.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A broad 2026-27 electoral wave across major member states compounds the risk that the EU never reaches consensus on China.
insights.quote: Looming elections in Italy, Spain and Poland also look set to weaken the appetite of those countries for bold EU initiatives.
insights.locator: This possibility has already led European capitals

insights.claim: In May 2026 China adopted regulations 834, 835 and 837 to protect its supply chains, counter extraterritorial measures against its companies, and monitor outbound investments.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Beijing is building a legal coercion toolkit faster than the EU implements its own, shifting the balance of leverage toward China.
insights.quote: it adopted regulations 834, 835 and 837 to better protect Chinese supply chains, counter extraterritorial measures against its companies, and monitor outbound investments.

insights.claim: Huawei's reported expansion of advanced chip production is eroding the leverage Europe derives from ASML's dominance in chipmaking equipment by making China less vulnerable to Western technology restrictions.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: If China's chip self-sufficiency advances, ASML-based export-control leverage weakens, a structural negative for the EU's strategic bargaining position.
insights.quote: potentially eroding the leverage Europe derives from Dutch company ASML’s dominance in chip making equipment.
insights.locator: Huawei’s reported expansion of advanced chip production

insights.claim: China's rare-earth export-licensing regime requires European companies to disclose their operations, customers and supply chains, giving Beijing a detailed map of Europe's vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The licensing regime doubles as an intelligence tool, increasing China's ability to target precise European chokepoints with future coercion.
insights.quote: Its rare-earth export-licensing regime, for example, requires European companies to provide details about their operations, customers and supply chains.
insights.locator: At the same time, Beijing is gaining a better picture

insights.claim: New EU proposals—the Industrial Accelerator Act with 'Buy European' provisions, a Cybersecurity Act revision restricting Chinese high-risk tech, and a possible 'diversification instrument'—go beyond case-by-case trade defense to target China's whole export-led model.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: These structural tools, if enacted, would materially restrict Chinese market access in the EU; watch the October Council and the IAA's progress.
insights.quote: The draft Industrial Accelerator Act (IAA), for example, contains “Buy European” provisions
insights.locator: Thirdly, EU leaders should draw confidence

insights.claim: China's Ministry of Commerce has formally objected to the new EU proposals—calling the IAA 'a serious investment barrier', demanding changes to the Cybersecurity Act, lobbying European business groups and planning a visit by Commerce Minister Wang Wentao—which the author reads as evidence the EU has real leverage.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Beijing's sharp reaction is a tell that the proposed tools bite; the intensity of Chinese lobbying is a real-time signal of which measures matter most.
insights.quote: The fact that China is clearly taking notice shows that the EU has meaningful leverage on this front.
insights.locator: The Chinese Ministry of Commerce has sent written objections
